stem:
Trace the following procedure:
\[ x = [[x0]] \]
\[ \textbf{repeat } [[n]] \textbf{ times}: \]
\[ \quad x = [[a]]\cdot x + ([[b]]) \]
What value does \( x \) hold after the procedure finishes?
solution: Apply the update \( x \mapsto [[a]]x+([[b]]) \) starting from \( x=[[x0]] \), a total of [[n]] times, to get \( x=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is off by one from the true final value.
This is off by one from the true final value in the other direction.
This adds one extra multiplication by [[a]] without the matching additions.
This subtracts the update constant once instead of applying the full update chain.
This stops after only ONE application of the update instead of [[n]].
This adds an unrelated adjustment of [[n]] to the correct final value.

stem:
Trace the following procedure:
\[ s = 0 \]
\[ \textbf{for } i = 1 \textbf{ to } [[n]]: \]
\[ \quad s = s + ([[c]]\cdot i + ([[d]])) \]
What value does \( s \) hold after the loop finishes?
solution: The accumulator sums \( [[c]]i+([[d]]) \) for \( i=1,\dots,[[n]] \): \( s=\sum_{i=1}^{[[n]]} ([[c]]i+([[d]])) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds one extra term's worth of drift to the correct sum.
This subtracts one extra term's worth of drift from the correct sum.
This drops the constant offset [[d]] added on every iteration.
This drops the [[c]]\cdot i term and only accumulates the constant offset.
This adds an unrelated adjustment of [[c]] to the correct total.
This subtracts an unrelated adjustment of [[d]] from the correct total.

stem:
Trace the following procedure:
\[ p = [[p0]] \]
\[ \textbf{for } i = 1 \textbf{ to } [[n]]: \]
\[ \quad p = p \cdot [[r]] \]
What value does \( p \) hold after the loop finishes?
solution: Each pass multiplies \( p \) by [[r]], so after [[n]] passes \( p=[[p0]]\cdot[[r]]^{[[n]]}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This stops one multiplication short of the correct count.
This applies one extra multiplication beyond the correct count.
This confuses repeated multiplication with repeated addition.
This forgets to include the initial value [[p0]] as a factor.
This adds an unrelated adjustment of [[r]] to the correct final value.
This is off by one from the true final value.

stem:
Trace the following procedure:
\[ x = [[x0]] \]
\[ \textbf{repeat } [[n]] \textbf{ times}: \]
\[ \quad x = ([[a]]\cdot x + [[b]]) \bmod [[m]] \]
What value does \( x \) hold after the procedure finishes? (Give the result as an integer in \( \{0,1,\dots,[[m]]-1\} \).)
solution: Apply \( x \mapsto ([[a]]x+[[b]]) \bmod [[m]] \) starting from \( x=[[x0]] \), [[n]] times, to get \( x=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is off by one (mod [[m]]) from the true final value.
This is off by one (mod [[m]]) from the true final value in the other direction.
This stops after only ONE application of the update instead of [[n]].
This is off by two (mod [[m]]) from the true final value.
This forgets to reduce mod [[m]] after a single update.
This adds an unrelated adjustment of [[a]] (mod [[m]]) to the correct final value.

stem:
Trace the following procedure:
\[ p = 1 \]
\[ \textbf{for } i = 1 \textbf{ to } [[n]]: \]
\[ \quad p = p \cdot i \]
\[ \textbf{return } p + ([[c]]) \]
What value does the procedure return?
solution: The loop computes \( p=[[n]]! \), so the procedure returns \( [[n]]! + ([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to add the constant offset [[c]] at the end.
This stops the factorial product one multiplication short.
This applies one extra multiplication beyond \( [[n]]! \).
This confuses the returned sum with an unrelated product of [[n]] and [[c]].
This is off by one from the true return value.
This subtracts [[c]] twice instead of adding it once.
